original_hypothesis.title: Auction DoS via Bid Array Gas Bloat Exceeding Block Gas Limit in claimAuction
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: An attacker places thousands of incrementally increasing micro-bids to inflate the auctionInfoData array. claimAuction iterates over ALL entries (including cancelled ones), and with sufficient entries the gas cost exceeds the block gas limit, making the auction permanently unclaimable and locking all bidder funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: participateToAuction only requires msg.value > returnHighestBid. Starting from 0, an attacker can bid 1 wei, 2 wei, 3 wei, etc. Each bid is pushed to the array. claimAuction's for-loop reads storage for every entry. Even cancelled entries consume gas for the iteration and storage reads.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i=0; i< auctionInfoData[_tokenid].length; i++) iterates all bids with storage reads and potential ETH transfers per entry. No pagination or gas limit protection.

preconditions: Block gas limit is finite (~30M on Ethereum mainnet)
Attacker has minimal ETH for thousands of micro-bids

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Each array entry requires ~3 SLOAD (bidder, bid, status) = ~6000 gas. 5000 entries = 30M gas minimum, approaching block limit.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Bid amounts: 1, 2, 3, ..., n wei. Total capital: n*(n+1)/2 wei. For n=5000: ~12.5M wei ≈ 0.0000125 ETH. Attacker can cancel bids to recover capital.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing: permanently locks legitimate bidders' ETH. Cost to attacker is primarily gas fees for placing/cancelling bids.

economic_questions: Exact gas cost per iteration in claimAuction including ETH transfer overhead?
How many entries needed to exceed the 30M block gas limit?
Can attacker recoup bid capital by cancelling individual bids?
validation_requirements: Deploy auction, place n bids, measure gas for claimAuction
Find the threshold n where claimAuction exceeds block gas limit
Estimate total gas cost for placing n bids

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.0001 ETH in bid capital (recoverable) + ~2-5 ETH in gas for placing 5000+ bids
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~2-5 ETH gas at current mainnet prices
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; griefing permanently locks all legitimate bidder ETH
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Hours to place bids; permanent lock afterward

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Start early in auction to fill array before legitimate bidders
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Target auction permanently uncollectable; all bid ETH locked

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate bidders for the targeted auction
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total value of all legitimate bids

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Block gas limit: 30M. Storage read cost: 2100 gas (cold), 100 gas (warm).
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Precise gas profiling needed for claimAuction iteration

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded array iteration DoS in claimAuction
validation_result.impact_summary: An attacker can place thousands of incrementally increasing micro-bids to inflate auctionInfoData, causing claimAuction to exceed the block gas limit. This permanently locks all bidder ETH and the auctioned NFT.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The attack exploits the fact that participateToAuction unconditionally pushes to the auctionInfoData[_tokenid] array with no size limit, and claimAuction performs multiple full iterations over this array (4 passes total: WinnerOrAdminRequired modifier calls returnHighestBidder, then returnHighestBid, then returnHighestBidder again, then the main for-loop). Each pass reads storage slots for every entry.

The attacker's strategy: bid 1 wei, bid 2 wei, cancel first bid (recover 1 wei), bid 3 wei, cancel second bid (recover 2 wei), etc. Each cancelled entry remains in the array with status=false. The attacker's net capital at any point is just the current highest bid, while the array grows unboundedly.

The attacker is limited by the fact that participateToAuction calls returnHighestBid (1 pass). At ~7000 entries, even placing a new bid approaches the 30M block gas limit. However, claimAuction with its 4 passes plus NFT transfer, ETH transfers, and event emissions becomes unexecutable at a much lower threshold (~4000-5000 entries).

Critically, there is no alternative mechanism to settle the auction or refund bidders. cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime, so it's unavailable after the auction ends. There's no admin rescue function. All ETH and the NFT are permanently locked.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows: (1) unbounded array growth in participateToAuction, (2) multiple full-array iterations in claimAuction with no pagination, (3) no array size cap, (4) no alternative settlement mechanism. The cancelled entries (status=false) still consume gas for storage reads and conditional checks during iteration.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No limit on array size. Each call appends a new entry. The require only checks bid amount, timing, and auction status - not array length. Combined with cancelBid for capital recovery, attacker can grow array cheaply.

Performs 4 full iterations: WinnerOrAdminRequired modifier calls returnHighestBidder (pass 1), then returnHighestBid (pass 2), returnHighestBidder (pass 3), and the main for-loop (pass 4). First pass is cold storage reads (~4200 gas per entry). With ~5000+ entries, total gas exceeds 30M block limit.

cancelBid sets status=false but does NOT remove the entry from the array. Cancelled entries remain and still consume gas during claimAuction iteration. Also, cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime, so it cannot be used as a refund mechanism after the auction ends.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls participateToAuction with 1 wei - array grows to length 1
2. Attacker calls participateToAuction with 2 wei - array grows to length 2
3. Attacker calls cancelBid(tokenid, 0) - recovers 1 wei, entry 0 status=false, array length still 2
4. Repeat: bid N+1 wei, cancel previous bid at index N-1, recover N wei. Net cost per iteration is gas only.
5. After ~5000 iterations, array has ~5000 entries (most with status=false)
7. WinnerOrAdminRequired modifier calls returnHighestBidder - iterates all 5000 entries (cold reads: ~21M gas)
8. returnHighestBid called - iterates 5000 entries (warm reads: ~1M gas)
9. returnHighestBidder called again - iterates 5000 entries (warm: ~1M gas)
10. Main for-loop iterates 5000 entries - reads + conditional logic (~1M gas)
11. Total: ~24-30M gas, exceeding or approaching block gas limit
12. Transaction reverts. No alternative settlement path exists. Funds permanently locked.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: participateToAuction pushes to unbounded array (line 60). claimAuction iterates the full array 4 times via returnHighestBidder (modifier, line 104), returnHighestBid (line 107), returnHighestBidder (line 109), and the main for-loop (line 110). Each of these functions at AuctionDemo.sol lines 65-86 and 89-100 contain unbounded for-loops over auctionInfoData[_tokenid]. With sufficient entries (~5000+), the combined gas cost of cold storage reads (2100 gas per slot, ~2 slots per entry) across 4 passes exceeds 30M block gas limit.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts for AuctionDemo provided in fixturesDeployment.js. The vulnerability is inherent to the contract code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is feasible with minimal capital. Starting from 0 (returnHighestBid returns 0 when no bids exist), the attacker bids 1 wei, then incrementally higher. By cancelling previous bids, the attacker recovers capital and only pays gas fees. At ~15 gwei gas price, each participateToAuction costs approximately 100K-200K gas initially (~0.003 ETH), increasing as the array grows. For 5000 bids, the average cost per bid is roughly 0.02 ETH (accounting for growing returnHighestBid iteration cost). Total attack cost: ~100 ETH in gas for 5000 bids on mainnet. On L2s or during low gas periods, this would be significantly cheaper. The attack permanently locks all legitimate bidder funds.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL - The attack permanently locks all ETH deposited by legitimate bidders and prevents the NFT from being transferred to the winner. There is no rescue mechanism: cancelBid is disabled after auction end, there is no admin withdrawal function on AuctionDemo, and claimAuction is the only settlement path. The NFT remains with the original owner but cannot be re-auctioned for the same tokenid (auctionClaim is never set to true, but the auction status check would need to be reset by admin on the minter). All losing bidder ETH is permanently frozen.
recommended_mitigations: Add a maximum bid count per auction (e.g., require(auctionInfoData[_tokenid].length < MAX_BIDS))
Implement a pull-based refund pattern where losing bidders withdraw their own funds individually, rather than iterating in claimAuction
Require a minimum bid increment (e.g., 0.01 ETH or 5% above current highest) to make array bloat economically expensive
Add pagination to claimAuction allowing it to process refunds in batches across multiple transactions
Add an admin emergency refund function that can process refunds in batches if claimAuction fails
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AuctionDemo with mock minter and gencore contracts. 2. Create an auction with a long end time. 3. Use a script to place N incrementally increasing bids (1 wei, 2 wei, ...), cancelling previous bids to recover capital. 4. Measure gas consumption of claimAuction at various N values (100, 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, 5000). 5. Identify the threshold N where claimAuction gas exceeds 30M. 6. Verify that after this threshold, claimAuction reverts with out-of-gas and funds are permanently locked.
uncertainties: Exact gas cost per iteration depends on Solidity compiler optimizations and EVM implementation specifics
The struct packing of auctionInfoStru affects how many storage slots are read per entry (2 or 3 slots)

The natural limit imposed by returnHighestBid in participateToAuction means the attacker hits their own gas limit before claimAuction becomes completely impossible - the exact crossover point needs empirical testing
On L2s with higher effective gas limits, more entries may be needed but cost per bid is lower
additional_testing_needed: Empirical gas measurement of claimAuction at various array sizes to find exact DoS threshold
Gas profiling of participateToAuction to determine maximum achievable array size
Testing on a fork of mainnet with actual block gas limit constraints
Verify struct storage layout to determine exact number of SLOAD operations per entry
